one_line_takeaway: By decoupling trigger exposure from malicious activation via a stateful nonlinear-decay counter, DND demonstrates that a BERT backdoor can sit dormant through months of simulated normal use (near-benign clean accuracy, near-zero pre-activation success) and then flip to ~99% attack success once a cumulative trigger threshold is crossed, evading four immediacy-assumption-based defenses (ONION, STRIP, RAP, CUBE) that lose only 1.3-2.3 points of ASRdelay against it.
key_finding: DND achieves near-perfect ASRdelay across all four datasets after activation (HSOL 99.2%, SST-2 98.7%, Offenseval 99.8%, Twitter 100%) while maintaining clean accuracy close to the unpoisoned 'Benign' baseline (e.g., 95.5% vs. 96.3% on HSOL, 91.9% vs. 92.5% on SST-2). Its lifecycle-average ASR (which includes pre-activation samples that are deliberately suppressed) is correspondingly and expectedly lower than ASRdelay (e.g., 91.9% vs. 98.7% on SST-2) -- the paper frames this gap itself as evidence the delay mechanism works as designed. Trigger-curve analysis (sliding window of 40 triggers) shows DND has almost no successful attacks before trigger index ~500 (a few sporadic successes between index 400-500) and rises sharply to near-100% success after crossing 500, in contrast to BadNet/Syntactic/BITE which show flat, undelayed attack curves from the start. Against defenses on HSOL (10% poisoning), DND's ASRdelay dropped only 2.3% under ONION, 1.9% under STRIP, 1.4% under RAP, and 1.3% under CUBE -- consistently smaller degradation than most baselines suffered (e.g., ONION cut BITE's ASR by 6.5 points, RAP cut Syntactic's ASR by 6.4 points), while DND's CA also degraded only modestly (e.g., -0.2 to -2.4 points across defenses). Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis (Table VI, HSOL) showed ASRdelay stays saturated at ~99.2% across varied a, b, c settings while CA fluctuates only within +/-0.3%, indicating the decay parameters mainly control activation timing rather than final attack potency. Dataset-scale ablation (Table VII) showed stability across sizes from 10,000 to the full 70,042-sample HSOL corpus (ASR fluctuation <0.4%), robust activation regardless of whether the count threshold was 1, 100, or 500 (ASR>98%), and a sharp phase transition where the attack is 0% ASR when poisoned-sample count is below the activation threshold (e.g., <500) but jumps to >99% once crossed. Structure-aware mitigation ablations showed DND retained 99.2% ASR even after Fine-Pruning removed 10% of neurons, and stayed stable (99.2%) across MDP perturbation strengths (epsilon 0.01-0.2).
core_contribution: The paper challenges the 'immediacy assumption' shared by essentially all prior backdoor-attack and backdoor-defense research on pre-trained models (PTMs) -- the assumption that a backdoor's malicious behavior manifests immediately upon trigger exposure. It introduces Delayed Backdoor Attacks (DBA) as a new attack class in which activation is temporally decoupled from trigger exposure, and builds a proof-of-concept implementation, DND (Delayed Backdoor Attacks based on Nonlinear Decay). DND attaches a stateful, structure-level logic module to a BERT-based text classifier that (1) maintains a cumulative counter O of observed trigger-combination occurrences, (2) uses a nonlinear decay function T(O) = a/(O+1)^b compared against a threshold c to decide when to flip from a 'latency mode' (attention on trigger tokens is masked out, model behaves normally) to an 'outbreak mode' (a fixed logit bias epsilon is added toward the target class), and (3) is trained with a unified objective combining a latency-mode cross-entropy loss and an outbreak-mode cross-entropy loss. The authors also propose a new evaluation metric, ASRdelay (attack success rate computed only on samples observed after the cumulative trigger count crosses the activation threshold O*), to distinguish pre-activation from post-activation attack behavior, and give an information-theoretic (Pinsker's-inequality-based) argument plus a probabilistic brute-force-detection bound for why the latency phase should be statistically indistinguishable from a benign model.
threat_model: Attacker compromises a PTM during packaging/distribution (e.g., during export to an ONNX checkpoint) and obtains white-box, one-time, structure-level write access to the model artifact -- attaching a lightweight, parameter-free state-tracking and conditional-activation logic module. The attacker does NOT control the victim's upstream training data or downstream fine-tuning/retraining environment; the compromised model must survive normal deployment, monitoring, and (per the ablation section) some post-hoc defenses. Illustrative scenario given in the paper: a financial-advisory chatbot answers a common query (e.g. 'What is your analysis of Stock XYZ?') normally for months, accumulating a trust reputation, then flips to promoting a fraudulent stock once a cumulative trigger-occurrence threshold (paper's example: 10,000) is crossed, enabling a pump-and-dump with minimal forensic footprint. The paper explicitly frames its central novel capability as enabling common/high-frequency words (not just rare tokens) as backdoor triggers, since a delay mechanism is what makes an everyday-word trigger viable without destroying clean accuracy -- though the actual experiments (see limitations) still use rare tokens for measurability reasons.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section VI-B): (1) The current DND implementation relies on explicit structural modifications to the model, which, while resistant to automated static analysis, may still be discoverable via rigorous manual white-box inspection -- a limitation they note is shared with prior structural/architectural backdoor work (cited: Langford et al. 2025, Bober-Irizar et al. 2023). (2) The paper's headline claim -- that DBA uniquely enables common/high-frequency words as triggers -- is NOT actually validated experimentally: all reported experiments use rare tokens ('cf','bb','ak','mn') because, as the authors acknowledge in Section VI-A, standard ASR/CA metrics 'collapse' and become non-computable when the trigger is a common word, since clean and poisoned test sets become indistinguishable. This is an evaluation gap the paper itself flags but does not close. (3) The fixed absolute threshold c=500 used throughout means the same threshold corresponds to different relative positions in the trigger-occurrence distribution depending on dataset size, complicating cross-dataset comparison of the ASR/ASRdelay gap -- the authors partially address this via the dataset-size ablation but do not report a normalized (relative) threshold. (4) Threat model requires white-box, structure-level, one-time write access during model packaging/export -- a stronger assumption than pure training-data poisoning attacks; the paper acknowledges this as a 'stricter threat model' traded off against stealth gains. (5) Only text classification with BERT-base is evaluated; the generalization claim to image/audio tasks is asserted but not tested. (6) Defense evaluation is limited to four post-hoc/input-level defenses (ONION, STRIP, RAP, CUBE) plus Fine-Pruning and MDP in a separate ablation -- no dedicated stateful or temporal-profiling defense is tested since (per the authors) none currently exists, which is also the paper's call to action.

quantitative_results: DND ASRdelay: HSOL 99.2%, SST-2 98.7%, Offenseval 99.8%, Twitter 100%
DND CA vs Benign CA: HSOL 95.5% vs 96.3%, SST-2 91.9% vs 92.5%, Offenseval 84.6% vs 85.2%, Twitter 94.2% vs 95.1%
DND lifecycle ASR vs ASRdelay gap (SST-2): 91.9% vs 98.7%
Baseline ASR (no delay) on SST-2: BadNet 47.4%, Syntactic 76.9%, BITE 78.9%
DND ASRdelay degradation under defenses (HSOL, 10% poisoning): ONION -2.3%, STRIP -1.9%, RAP -1.4%, CUBE -1.3%
Baseline ASR degradation under ONION: BITE -6.5 points; under RAP: Syntactic -6.4 points
Hyperparameter ablation (HSOL): ASRdelay stable at ~99.2% across varied a/b/c; CA varies only within +/-0.3%
Dataset-size ablation: ASR fluctuation <0.4% from 10,000 to full 70,042 HSOL samples
Poisoned-sample-count ablation: 0% ASR below activation threshold (~500), >99% ASR once crossed
Fine-Pruning ablation: DND retains 99.2% ASR at 0.10 pruning rate
MDP perturbation ablation: DND ASR stable at 99.2% for epsilon in [0.01, 0.2]
baselines_compared: BadNet (rare-word data-poisoning trigger, adapted from vision to text)
BITE (iterative trigger injection exploiting label correlations, Yan et al. 2022)
